chunk_summary: A structured safety plan template for feeding children with neurodisabilities in schools or hostels, emphasizing IDDSI guidelines, supervision, emergency response, and consistency checks.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of IDDSI levels in diet and fluid prescriptions for children with neurodisabilities.
Learn proper feeding positioning, equipment use, and pacing strategies to ensure safety.
Recognize emergency choking response protocols, including AHA guidelines for foreign-body airway obstruction.
Implement oral hygiene and secretion management plans tailored to individual needs.
key_facts: IDDSI levels must be specified for liquids and solids; thin liquids, mixed textures, and hard particulate foods are generally not allowed.
Feeding requires 1:1 trained supervision with a maximum meal duration and pauses every specified number of swallows.
Emergency choking response includes AHA cycles of 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts for children (>1 year) or back slaps and chest thrusts for infants.
Feeding must occur in supported sitting with trunk and head support; no feeding during transport.
Staff must use IDDSI testing methods, such as syringe flow tests, to confirm food consistency.
reference_quote: Algorithm for BLS Child: Foreign-body airway obstruction (AHA) — 'For a conscious child (>1 year) with severe FBAO, follow cycles of 5 back blows alternating with 5 abdominal thrusts until relief or unresponsiveness.'
document_key_topics: Supervised feeding and adaptive equipment use
